name: Courchevel
intro: Courchevel is the grande dame of the French Alps, and for many the most polished ski address in the world. It is really four villages climbing the mountainside, from wooded Le Praz at 1,300m through 1550 and 1650 to the celebrated 1850 at the top, each with its own character but all sharing an unmatched concentration of Michelin-starred restaurants, designer boutiques and private-jet arrivals at the mountain altiport. What lifts it above mere glamour is the skiing: Courchevel sits at one end of the Trois Vallees, the largest linked ski area on earth, so the sense of space is as remarkable as the sense of occasion.
skiing: Courchevel's home slopes are famous for their immaculate grooming and their generous, confidence-building blues above the tree line, which makes it one of the finest resorts anywhere for families and improving skiers. There is far more to it than gentle cruising, though: the steeper couloirs of the Saulire and the tree runs down to Le Praz give strong skiers plenty to think about, and the resort has real off-piste pedigree. From the top lifts the whole of the Trois Vallees opens up, some 600km of connected pistes across to Meribel and Val Thorens, comfortably more than a week's skiing without ever repeating yourself. It is a mountain that flatters every level while still rewarding the expert.
town: Where you stay in Courchevel matters more than in almost any other resort, because the four villages are genuinely different places. 1850 is the glamorous heart, ski-in and ski-out and lined with the grand hotels and boutiques; 1650 (Moriond) is sunnier, more relaxed and better value; 1550 is quieter and family-minded; and Le Praz, down among the trees, keeps a village feel that the higher stations lack. The standard of chalet here is as high as anywhere in the Alps, and the best properties are ski-in, ski-out flagships of real ambition. Our own Courchevel collection is deliberately short and weighted towards 1850 and the calm of Le Praz, and we match the address to the group.
when: Courchevel's season runs from early December to late April, and its altitude and largely north-facing aspect give it a dependable snow record across those months. The depth of winter brings the fullest social scene and the busiest weeks, particularly around the holidays, while the longer, brighter days of spring bring sun to the terraces and a gentler pace to the resort. As part of the high Trois Vallees the skiing holds up well into the season. Rather than single out a particular week, we would rather understand your group, the balance of abilities and the appetite for scene versus quiet, and steer you to the timing that suits.
getting: Courchevel is well served by air and, for a high resort, unusually quick to reach. Most guests fly into Geneva, a little over two hours away by road, or Chambery and Lyon, both around two hours, with the final climb from Moutiers up the switchbacks into the valley. The resort also has its own altiport, cut into the mountainside above 1850, which takes light aircraft and helicopters directly and is a spectacle in its own right. For a rail arrival, Moutiers is the nearest station, with transfers up from there. We arrange every leg, including an altiport or helicopter transfer when time is short.
beyond: Courchevel gives non-skiers and rest days as much as it gives the skiing. The boutiques of 1850 are a destination in themselves, there are hotel spas of real quality open to non-residents, and simply watching the light aircraft land on the mountainside altiport is a pleasure. Beyond that there is a toboggan run, winter walking trails between the villages, an ice rink and paragliding for the more adventurous. The scenery and the sense of theatre carry the quieter days.
eating: Nowhere in the Alps eats better than Courchevel, which carries a remarkable density of Michelin stars alongside its mountain restaurants and long, sunny lunch terraces. On the hill, the rifugi and pistes-side tables reward a slow lunch; in the villages, the range runs from unfussy Savoyard cooking to some of the most celebrated dining rooms in France. The best of them book out months ahead. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and secure the tables that are otherwise impossible to get.
pick: Courchevel rewards a group that plans a little. Base yourself in 1850 for the ski-in ease and the evenings, or Le Praz for calm and character, keep one long lunch on the mountain and one great table in the village, and leave the impossible reservations and the altiport transfer to us.

name: Val d'Isere
intro: Val d'Isere is one of the great skiers' resorts of the world, a place where serious mountains meet a village that stays genuinely alive long after the lifts stop. Linked with neighbouring Tignes to form the vast Espace Killy, it offers high, snow-sure terrain that holds its quality from the opening weeks of the season to the very end, and a reputation for demanding skiing that has drawn the committed for generations. The village itself is handsome and unusually lively, with good shops, better restaurants and an apres scene that needs no persuading, which is a rare combination at this altitude.
skiing: The skiing is the heart of Val d'Isere, and it has genuine range. The Face de Bellevarde, the Solaise and the terrain off the Pissaillas glacier give experienced skiers and off-piste enthusiasts some of the most rewarding descents in the Alps, while gentler sectors such as Le Fornet and the wide runs above the village suit those still finding their feet. Because so much of the Espace Killy sits high, the 300km of linked pistes hold their snow later than almost anywhere in France, which makes it a dependable choice at both ends of the season. It is a resort that flatters the confident and gives the expert plenty to chase.
town: Val d'Isere is a real village rather than a purpose-built station, and that character is much of its appeal. The centre, rebuilt in a handsome traditional style, puts the best of the shops, restaurants and nightlife within walking distance, while quieter corners such as La Daille and Le Fornet trade a little convenience for calm. Where you base yourself shapes the week, between the buzz of the centre and the peace of the outskirts, and the standard of chalet here is among the highest in France. Our own collection favours the central village and the sought-after slopes above it, and we match the property to how the group intends to ski.
when: Val d'Isere's season runs from late November to early May, one of the longest in the Alps, thanks to its altitude and its glacier. The high, snow-sure terrain makes it a strong choice for an early or late-season trip when lower resorts are anxious about cover; the depth of winter brings the liveliest weeks and the fullest programme; and the long spring days bring sun to the terraces and superb conditions up high. Rather than name a single best week, we would rather understand your group and the kind of week they want, and match the timing to it.
getting: Val d'Isere sits high at the head of its valley, so the transfer is a little longer than for some resorts, but it is straightforward from three major airports. Geneva is the usual arrival, around three hours away by road, with Chambery and Lyon the alternatives, each a touch under. The final approach climbs the Tarentaise valley, and the nearest railway station is Bourg-Saint-Maurice, with transfers up from there. We arrange every leg, and can add a helicopter transfer from the valley floor when you would rather skip the last of the climb.
beyond: There is plenty to a week in Val d'Isere beyond the skiing. When the lifts stop the village keeps going, but there is calm to be found too, with hotel spas, ski-touring and snowshoe trails away from the crowds, and a smart run of shops to browse. Guided off-piste and heli-drops in Italy are a particular draw for the adventurous, while the ice rink, the pool and gentle winter walks look after quieter days and non-skiers. The apres, when you want it, is among the best known in the Alps.
eating: Val d'Isere takes its eating seriously, from long, sunny lunches on the mountain terraces to a village that delivers everything from honest Savoyard cooking to genuinely ambitious dinners. The mountain huts reward a slow midday stop, and the centre has real range for the evening, with the apres running from easy to legendary just outside the door. The best tables fill quickly in the busy weeks. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the ones that are hardest to get.
pick: Val d'Isere rewards the keen. Ski hard off Bellevarde and the Solaise in the mornings while the snow is best, take a long lunch on a mountain terrace, and give one evening to the village, which does apres and dinner as well as any resort in the Alps. The transfer, the guides and the tables, we will handle.

name: Meribel
intro: Meribel sits in the middle of the Trois Vallees, which means the largest linked ski area in the world spreads out in every direction from the door. Built to a strict chalet-style code in a beautiful wooded valley, it is one of the prettiest resorts in the French Alps and has long been a favourite of those who take both their skiing and their week in a chalet seriously. Its central position and its British-founded history give it an easy, welcoming feel, and it manages to be both a serious mountain base and a genuinely charming village.
skiing: From Meribel you sit at the hub of 600km of linked pistes, and can be in Courchevel or Val Thorens within the hour, with a huge spread of blues and reds in between that suits mixed-ability groups perfectly. The valley's own terrain runs from gentle, tree-lined runs back to the village, a real asset in poor visibility when higher resorts can be a wash-out, up to the steeper slopes and off-piste of the Mont Vallon and the Saulire. It is skiing built for covering ground and for keeping a varied group happy, with the whole of the Trois Vallees on a single pass.
town: Meribel's insistence on wood-and-stone chalet architecture gives it a coherence that many resorts lack, and its several levels each have their own feel. The main village and Meribel Village put you close to the lifts and the life, while the higher, quieter hamlets and the tree-lined slopes above trade a little bustle for peace and often the best chalet positions. The standard of property is high and chalet-focused, which is exactly why the valley has drawn a loyal following. Our own collection runs from the centre up to the calmer, wooded slopes above, and we match the address to the group.
when: Meribel's season runs from early December to late April, and as the heart of the high Trois Vallees it holds its snow well across those months. The holidays and the depth of winter bring the busiest, liveliest weeks, while the longer spring days bring sun to the terraces and a relaxed pace to the valley. The tree-lined runs are a particular comfort earlier in the season and in poor weather. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Meribel is one of the quicker high resorts to reach. Geneva and Chambery are both around two hours away by road, with Lyon a touch further, and the final climb comes up from Moutiers, the nearest railway station, which sits just below the valley. That short transfer from the valley floor is part of the appeal for families and mixed groups who would rather not spend half a day getting to resort. We arrange every transfer, and can add a helicopter leg from the valley when time is short.
beyond: Meribel looks after the non-skier and the rest day well. There is an Olympic ice rink, a good sports and wellness centre, gentle winter footpaths through the woods and a relaxed run of shops and cafes, all in a genuinely pretty setting. Spas, tobogganing and paragliding fill the more energetic afternoons, while the valley's good-natured, family-friendly mood makes it an easy place simply to potter. The whole of the Trois Vallees is a lift ride away when you want more.
eating: Meribel does easy, generous mountain cooking especially well, with sunny lunch terraces and welcoming village restaurants that suit families and long, unhurried days. The valley's dining is more relaxed than showy, though there is real quality to be found, and the wider Trois Vallees, Courchevel included, is within reach for a grander evening. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the tables worth the effort.
pick: Meribel rewards the group that likes to cover ground. Use its central position to roam the whole Trois Vallees, keep the tree-lined runs in your pocket for a bad-weather day, and settle into the valley's easy, chalet-first rhythm in the evenings. The reservations and the transfers, we will handle.

name: Chamonix
intro: Chamonix is unlike any other resort in the Alps, a proper year-round town wedged into a deep valley beneath the highest mountain in Western Europe. It is the birthplace of alpinism, and that heritage runs through everything: the skiing is spread across several distinct areas rather than one linked domain, and the town's reputation rests on the extraordinary off-piste, the Aiguille du Midi cable car and the legendary Vallee Blanche descent. For a certain kind of skier, and for anyone who loves real mountains and a real town, there is nowhere quite like it.
skiing: Chamonix's skiing is a collection of characterful, separate areas rather than a single joined-up domain, and it rewards those who embrace that. The Grands Montets above Argentiere is the classic advanced sector, north-facing and snow-holding, dropping away into legendary off-piste; Brevent-Flegere offers sunnier cruising with the whole Mont Blanc massif filling the view; and Le Tour and Les Houches give gentler, tree-lined terrain for mixed groups and poorer weather. Above it all is the Vallee Blanche, a glacier descent of more than 20km from the Aiguille du Midi that is a bucket-list day best taken with a guide. It is a valley that gives most to strong and adventurous skiers.
town: Being a genuine town, Chamonix offers a range of bases that no purpose-built resort can match. The town centre itself is lively and full of life, with the shops, bars and restaurants of a place that lives all year; the quieter hamlets up the valley, Argentiere especially, put you closer to the serious skiing with a calmer feel; and Les Houches at the lower end is gentler and family-minded. The chalet standard is high and the choice broad. Our own collection ranges from the town to the quieter valley hamlets, and we match the base to how the group wants to ski.
when: Chamonix's season runs from early December to early May, and the high, glaciated terrain holds its snow and its interest across those months. The depth of winter brings the coldest, most reliable snow on the north-facing Grands Montets, while the longer spring days are prized for the Vallee Blanche and the big glacier descents. As a real town, Chamonix has life whatever the season. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group, and the appetite for off-piste in particular, and match the timing to it.
getting: Chamonix is the closest major resort to Geneva, and that ease of access is a real part of its appeal. The airport is barely an hour and a quarter away by road, which makes it one of the simplest transfers in the Alps and a genuine option for a long weekend as well as a full week. The town has its own railway connections up the valley too, via St-Gervais. We arrange the car, and a helicopter leg when you want to arrive in style, though for Chamonix the road transfer is quick enough that most guests do not need it.
beyond: Even without skis, Chamonix is extraordinary. The Aiguille du Midi cable car and the Montenvers railway to the Mer de Glace are unforgettable, there is skating, paragliding and a good clutch of spas, and the town's alpine museums tell the story of the birthplace of mountaineering. As a real town it has genuine shopping, bars and life beyond the resort bubble, and the scenery from every viewpoint is on a scale that few places can match. Non-skiers and rest days are richly served.
eating: Being a real town, Chamonix has genuine range at the table, from Savoyard institutions and mountain huts to modern bistros and a lively bar scene that owes as much to the town as to the slopes. The mountain restaurants reward a long lunch with a Mont Blanc view, and the evening choice is broad and unpretentious. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the tables worth planning around.
pick: Chamonix rewards the adventurous. Give a clear day to the Vallee Blanche with a guide, chase cold snow on the Grands Montets, and enjoy the town in the evening, because Chamonix lives all year in a way ski resorts rarely do. The guides, the timings and the tables, we will handle.

name: Megeve
intro: Megeve was created as a French answer to St Moritz, and it has never lost its poise. The cobbled medieval centre, with its horse-drawn sleighs, its family-run boutiques and its patisseries, sits below a broad, gentle ski area, and the whole place is wrapped in an unhurried, deeply chic atmosphere that owes as much to gastronomy and style as to skiing. It is a resort for those who want elegance and a beautiful village as much as vertical, and it does that combination as well as anywhere in the Alps.
skiing: Megeve's skiing is spread over three linked, mostly gentle and tree-lined sectors, with the Mont Blanc massif as a constant backdrop, and it is close to ideal for families, cruisers and improving skiers. It is not a resort for those chasing steeps or big off-piste, and it is honest about that; what it offers instead is a large, pretty, forgiving area where a mixed group can spread out and enjoy long, scenic runs without pressure. The wider Evasion Mont Blanc area gives plenty of ground to cover. For confidence and character over challenge, few resorts do it better.
town: Megeve's medieval heart is the real draw, a genuinely beautiful, car-limited village of cobbled streets, a fine old church and the daily theatre of sleighs and smart shopfronts. You can base yourself in or beside the centre for the shops, the restaurants and the atmosphere, or in a secluded farmhouse in the surrounding meadows for privacy and space. The chalet standard is very high, and Megeve's properties tend to be as much about comfort and style as about ski access. Our own collection leans into that elegance, and we match the address to the group.
when: Megeve's season runs from mid December to mid April. Its altitude is modest, so it relies on good snowmaking and cooler spells, but the tree-lined runs ski well in poor visibility and the resort's charm carries the quieter conditions. The holidays bring the fullest social scene, while the longer spring days suit long lunches and the resort's relaxed pace. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Megeve enjoys one of the shortest transfers in the Alps, which is a large part of its appeal for a long weekend as well as a full week. Geneva is around an hour away by road, an easy and scenic run, with the resort's own small altiport taking light aircraft directly. That closeness to Geneva makes arrivals and departures genuinely effortless. We arrange every transfer, with a helicopter option straight into resort for those who want it.
beyond: Megeve is made for the non-skier and the gentle day. The village is a pleasure simply to wander, with its designer boutiques, patisseries and hotel spas, while horse-drawn sleigh rides, winter walks and even hot-air ballooning over the massif give the resort its unhurried character. It is a place where the shopping, the eating and the setting are the point as much as the skiing. Non-skiers are thoroughly at home.
eating: For its size, Megeve eats extraordinarily well, with a gastronomic reputation out of all proportion to the resort and a run of cosy mountain restaurants serving impeccable Savoyard classics. The village carries celebrated fine dining alongside its patisseries and cafes, and the mountain terraces reward a long, sunny lunch. The best tables book out fastest. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and secure the ones worth the effort.
pick: Megeve rewards those who slow down. Cruise the gentle, scenic slopes with the massif in view, give the afternoons to the cobbled village and its shops, and treat the eating as seriously as the skiing, because here it deserves it. The sleighs, the spas and the tables, we will handle.

name: Tignes
intro: Tignes sits high above the tree line and skis with Val d'Isere across the vast Espace Killy, and its altitude and glacier make it one of the most snow-sure destinations in the Alps. It is a functional, high-mountain resort rather than a pretty village, and it is unapologetic about that; what it offers is reliable snow, a huge linked area and a season that runs longer than almost anywhere in France. For skiers who put snow-security and terrain first, it is hard to beat.
skiing: The Grande Motte glacier is Tignes's trump card, guaranteeing good snow when lower resorts struggle and giving long, high-altitude descents into the early summer. Beyond it, the resort shares 300km of varied terrain with Val d'Isere across the Espace Killy, with plenty for every level from wide beginner slopes to serious off-piste and steep couloirs. It is a strong choice for confident skiers and for early or late-season trips, and the sheer reliability of the snow underpins everything. The link to Val d'Isere doubles the sense of scale.
town: Tignes is spread across several high, purpose-built villages around a lake, and where you stay changes the feel of the week. Tignes le Lac and Val Claret put you closest to the lifts and the ski-in convenience, while Tignes les Brevieres, lower and older, keeps more of a village character. It is not a resort of great architectural charm, but the practicality is the point, and the best chalets make the most of the position and the snow. Our own collection favours the best-placed properties for quick access to the lifts and the Val d'Isere link.
when: Tignes has one of the longest seasons in the Alps, running from late autumn on the glacier right through to May, and its altitude gives it exceptional snow-security throughout. It is a natural choice for an early or late trip when snow elsewhere is uncertain, while the depth of winter brings the fullest resort. The high, treeless terrain can be exposed in poor weather, which is worth planning around. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: Tignes sits high at the head of the Tarentaise, so the transfer is a little longer, but the reward is the snow. Geneva and Chambery are both around three hours away by road, with Lyon a similar distance, and the final approach climbs past Val d'Isere's valley to the resort. The nearest railway station is Bourg-Saint-Maurice, with transfers up from there. We arrange every leg, and can add a helicopter transfer from the valley floor when you would rather rise above the switchbacks.
beyond: Beyond the pistes, Tignes offers a distinctive set of diversions that suit its high, snow-sure setting. There is ice diving beneath the frozen lake, ice-driving circuits, one of France's best-regarded terrain parks and a large sports centre, while winter walking trails give quieter souls space to breathe. It is more about activity than atmosphere, and the neighbouring charm of Val d'Isere is a short hop away when you want it. Non-skiers who like to be active are well served.
eating: Tignes keeps its dining honest and hearty, with welcoming village restaurants and sunny mountain lunch stops that suit long days on the snow. It is not a gastronomic destination in the way of some resorts, but the cooking is generous and the more refined tables of Val d'Isere are within easy reach for a grander evening. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book whatever suits the day.
pick: Tignes rewards the snow-focused. Lean on the Grande Motte glacier for reliable conditions, roam the whole Espace Killy with Val d'Isere, and nip over to its prettier neighbour for a grander dinner when the mood takes. The transfer and the tables, we will handle.

name: Val Thorens
intro: At 2,300m, Val Thorens is the highest ski resort in Europe, which means snow when everywhere else is anxious about it. It is a true ski-in, ski-out village, compact and purpose-built, and it sits at the very top of the Trois Vallees with the whole of the largest linked ski area in the world falling away beneath it. It trades village charm for altitude and convenience, and for skiers who want guaranteed snow and terrain on the doorstep, that is a trade well worth making.
skiing: The high, open bowls around Val Thorens hold their snow superbly and suit everyone from improvers to experts, with several glaciers extending the season at both ends and a genuine sense of high-alpine scale. From the village you can drop straight into the full 600km of the Trois Vallees, skiing across to Meribel and Courchevel and back in a day, which is an extraordinary spread of terrain from a single ski-in base. The snow-security is the headline, but the sheer size of the linked area is the substance. It is a resort built for skiers who like to ski a lot.
town: Val Thorens is compact and entirely ski-in, ski-out, which is its great practical strength: lifts and slopes are on the doorstep, and you never need a car or a bus once you arrive. It is a purpose-built resort rather than a traditional village, and the architecture is functional, but the best chalets make the most of the position, the views and the immediate ski access. For a group that wants to maximise time on the snow, nothing beats it. Our own collection is chosen for position and comfort, with the lifts and slopes right outside.
when: Val Thorens has one of the longest and most reliable seasons in the Alps, running from late November to early May, thanks to its altitude and its glaciers. It is a dependable choice at both ends of the season when lower resorts are uncertain, while the depth of winter brings the fullest, liveliest village. The high, open terrain can be exposed in bad weather, when dropping down into the tree-lined runs of Meribel is the answer. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: Val Thorens sits at the very top of its valley, so the transfer is a scenic climb, but it is straightforward from the main airports. Geneva and Chambery are around three hours away by road, with Lyon a similar distance, and the final ascent comes up from Moutiers, the nearest railway station. The height means the last stretch is a proper mountain drive. We arrange every leg, and can add a helicopter transfer when you would rather rise above the switchbacks.
beyond: For a purpose-built resort, Val Thorens keeps non-skiers and rest days better entertained than you might expect. There is a three-kilometre toboggan run, one of the highest zip lines in the world, a large sports and wellness centre and a lively, sociable atmosphere, while the apres is famously spirited. It is more about energy and activity than quiet charm, and the whole of the Trois Vallees is on the doorstep for those who want to keep skiing. Active non-skiers are well served.
eating: For a purpose-built resort at altitude, Val Thorens dines surprisingly well, from buzzing mountain restaurants to a genuinely notable fine-dining scene that has grown up alongside the apres. The mountain terraces reward a long, high-altitude lunch in the sun, and the village has real range for the evening. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and secure the tables that fill fastest.
pick: Val Thorens rewards the group that wants to ski hard and stay close. Use the ski-in, ski-out ease and the guaranteed snow to roam the whole Trois Vallees, drop into Meribel's trees when the weather closes in, and enjoy the resort's spirited evenings. The transfer and the tables, we will handle.

name: Les Arcs
intro: Les Arcs is a cluster of four purpose-built villages climbing the mountainside above Bourg-Saint-Maurice, designed from the outset so that you can ski from the door. Linked by cable car to neighbouring La Plagne, it forms Paradiski, one of the largest ski areas in the world, and it pairs genuine ski-in, ski-out convenience with a huge spread of varied terrain. It is a practical, snow-sure choice that suits families and mixed groups who value getting straight onto the snow.
skiing: Les Arcs is known for varied, uncrowded pistes and some serious off-piste, and the terrain climbs impressively from wooded runs at Arc 1600 to the high, open slopes above Arc 2000. Its signature is the Aiguille Rouge, a descent that drops over 2,000 vertical metres from top to bottom, one of the great runs in the Alps. The Vanoise Express cable car opens up the whole of La Plagne and the full 425km of Paradiski, so the sense of space is remarkable. It is a mountain that gives ski-in ease to families and real challenge to the strong.
town: Les Arcs is really four villages at different heights, from wooded Arc 1600 through 1800 and 1950 to high, snow-sure Arc 2000, each with its own feel but all built for ski-in, ski-out living. The higher stations put you closest to the best snow and the quickest lift access, while 1600 and 1800 have more of a settled, tree-lined feel. The architecture is modern and functional, and the best chalets make the most of the position and the views. Our own collection is chosen for ski access and comfort across the villages.
when: Les Arcs's season runs from mid December to late April, and its range of altitudes gives it a dependable snow record, with the higher villages holding their cover well. The holidays and the depth of winter bring the busiest weeks, while the longer spring days suit the sunny, open upper slopes. The wooded runs lower down are a comfort in poor visibility. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Les Arcs is unusually easy to reach for a high resort, thanks to the funicular that climbs directly from the valley. Geneva and Chambery are both around two and a half hours away by road, and the resort sits above Bourg-Saint-Maurice, which is on the rail network, with a Eurostar service in winter and a funicular up to Arc 1600, making a genuinely car-free trip feasible. That rail link is a real point of difference. We arrange every transfer, including the funicular or a helicopter leg when time is short.
beyond: Beyond the pistes, Les Arcs offers tobogganing, winter walking, a spa or two and the novelty of the funicular arrival from the valley, all in a snow-sure, family-friendly setting. It is more about active diversion than village atmosphere, and the emphasis is firmly on the skiing, but there is enough to fill a rest day or entertain a non-skier. The wider Paradiski area is always there for those who want to keep going. Families are especially well served.
eating: Les Arcs keeps its dining relaxed and easy-going, with each village offering welcoming restaurants and sunny lunch spots, and a handful of more ambitious tables worth seeking out. It is not a gastronomic destination in itself, but the mountain lunches are generous and the atmosphere friendly, well suited to families and long days on the snow. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book whatever fits the plan.
pick: Les Arcs rewards the group that wants to ski from the door. Use the ski-in, ski-out ease and the Paradiski link to cover huge ground, save the Aiguille Rouge for a clear day, and consider the car-free arrival by rail and funicular. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: La Plagne
intro: La Plagne is really ten villages in one, ranging from high, snow-sure altitude stations to pretty, traditional hamlets lower down the mountain. Linked by cable car to Les Arcs to form Paradiski, one of the largest ski areas in the world, it has a long-standing reputation as one of the most reliable and welcoming family resorts in the Alps. Its scale, its variety of bases and its gentle, confidence-building terrain make it a natural choice for a mixed group or a multi-generational week.
skiing: La Plagne's pistes are mostly wide and confidence-building, which makes it one of the best family and improver resorts in France, with the Bellecote glacier adding height and snow-security at the top. There is more to it than gentle cruising, though: the link to Les Arcs across Paradiski opens up steeper, more demanding terrain and serious off-piste for those who want it, and the full 425km of the combined area gives plenty of ground to cover. It is a resort that lets beginners and experts in the same group each have their day. The variety across ten villages and two linked areas is its great strength.
town: La Plagne's ten villages give an unusually wide choice of base, from the high, purpose-built altitude stations such as Plagne Centre and Belle Plagne, which offer the best ski-in access, to the prettier, more traditional lower hamlets like Montchavin and Champagny, which keep a village feel. Where you stay shapes the character of the week, between convenience and charm. The best chalets make the most of the position and the family-friendly ease. Our own collection favours the villages with the best mix of ski access and character.
when: La Plagne's season runs from mid December to late April, and its spread of altitudes, topped by the Bellecote glacier, gives it a dependable snow record. The holidays bring the busiest, most family-heavy weeks, while the longer spring days suit the sunny lower villages and gentle terrain. The higher stations hold their snow best late in the season. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: La Plagne is straightforward to reach from the main gateways. Geneva and Chambery are both around two and a half hours away by road, with Lyon a little further, and the nearest railway stations are Aime and Bourg-Saint-Maurice down in the valley, with transfers up to the villages. The choice of base affects the final climb, with the higher stations a longer drive up. We arrange every transfer, and a helicopter leg when time is short.
beyond: La Plagne is built for families off the snow as well as on it. Its headline diversion is the Olympic bobsleigh track, a genuine adrenaline hit open to guests, and there is tobogganing, winter walking and swimming for quieter afternoons, all across a set of villages designed with families in mind. It is practical and welcoming rather than glamorous, and non-skiers and children are exceptionally well catered for. The wider Paradiski area is there for those who want more.
eating: La Plagne keeps its dining easy-going and generous, with welcoming restaurants and sunny lunch terraces across its villages, well suited to families and relaxed days on the snow. It is not a gastronomic destination, but the mountain cooking is hearty and the atmosphere friendly, and the wider Paradiski area offers more for a grander evening. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and point you to the best of them.
pick: La Plagne rewards the family and the mixed group. Pick the base that suits your party, from high ski-in stations to pretty lower hamlets, use Paradiski for the bigger days, and give an afternoon to the Olympic bobsleigh run. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Verbier
intro: Verbier is where serious skiing meets a town that never quite goes to bed. Perched high on a sunny shoulder above the Rhone valley in the Swiss canton of Valais, it opens straight onto the vast 4 Vallees, with some of the most celebrated lift-served off-piste anywhere in the Alps, and hands you back at the end of the day to a village of long lunches, later nights and very good tables. It is a resort that draws the committed skier and the sociable in equal measure, and does both exceptionally well.
skiing: Verbier has long been a freeride mecca, and confident skiers get the most from the steep, snow-holding terrain around Mont-Fort, Tortin and Mont-Gele, which is among the best lift-served off-piste in the world. There is far more range than the reputation suggests, though: wide, quiet pistes off Les Attelas suit intermediates, the tree-lined runs at Bruson keep the day going when the weather closes in, and beginners are well looked after on the nursery slopes at Les Esserts. The 4 Vallees gives an enormous spread of linked terrain on a single pass. It is a mountain that rewards ambition while still catering to a mixed group.
town: Verbier is a real town rather than a purpose-built station, and where you stay shapes the week. The Medran side puts you closest to the main lift and to ski-in convenience, while quieter corners such as Les Esserts and Sonalon trade a few minutes' transfer for calm and a view. The standard of chalet here is among the highest in the Alps, and the best properties are ambitious, design-led flagships. Our own collection is deliberately short, from ski-in flagships to quieter boltholes, and we match the property to the group rather than the other way round.
when: Verbier's season runs from late November to late April, and its high, largely north-facing terrain holds its snow well across those months, making it dependable at both ends of the season. The depth of winter brings the liveliest weeks and the fullest programme, while the longer spring days bring sun to the terraces and a more relaxed pace. The events calendar, from the freeride finals to a summer music festival, is a draw in its own right. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: Verbier is one of the more accessible major resorts. Most guests arrive through Geneva, around two hours away by road, with Sion, down in the valley, closer still for private aircraft. The final approach climbs from Le Chable, at the foot of the mountain, by cable car or a short transfer up into the village. That quick, scenic ascent is part of the appeal. We arrange every leg, including an in-resort helicopter transfer when time is short.
beyond: There is plenty to a week in Verbier beyond the skiing. The village has excellent spas and quiet winter walking, while the more energetic can try snowshoeing, tobogganing or paragliding off the shoulder of the mountain. The resort's events are a genuine draw, from the freeride spectacle that lands each spring to a world-class music festival in summer. Non-skiers and rest days are very well served, and the town's life carries the quieter days.
eating: Verbier takes its long lunches and its late nights equally seriously. On the mountain, a handful of characterful huts reward a slow lunch in the sun; in the village, the range runs from unfussy Swiss cooking to a genuinely ambitious table or two, and the apres is among the best known in the Alps, from easy terraces to the full, loud legend on the main square. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the tables that are hardest to get.
pick: Verbier rewards a group that plans a little. Base yourself on the Medran side for the easy mornings, keep one long lunch on the mountain and one good evening for a proper table in the village, and leave an afternoon spare for the apres to find you. The rest, we will handle.

name: Zermatt
intro: Zermatt sits at the head of a Valais valley beneath the unmistakable pyramid of the Matterhorn, and it may be the most beautiful ski town in the world. It is car-free, a place of weathered timber and grand hotels reached by train for the final stretch, and its skiing climbs higher than anywhere else in the Alps and links across the border to Cervinia in Italy. The result is snow-security that runs almost year-round, and views that never lose their power however many times you ride the lifts.
skiing: Zermatt's three linked areas rise to the Matterhorn Glacier Paradise at 3,883m, the highest lift-served point in the Alps, giving long, sweeping runs and reliable snow well into late spring. The connection over the border to Cervinia adds a great sweep of sunny Italian pistes and a memorable cross-border lunch, so the combined area is enormous. There is terrain for every level, from gentle high-altitude cruising to serious off-piste and glacier routes best taken with a guide. The altitude and the scale together make it one of the most dependable and rewarding mountains in the Alps.
town: Zermatt is entirely car-free, and simply moving around town by electric taxi or on foot, with the Matterhorn at the end of the street, is part of the pleasure. The centre is lively and full of grand hotels, characterful old timber and good shops, while quieter corners up the hillsides offer calm and the best of the views. The chalet standard is very high, and the best properties frame the mountain from the window. Our own collection favours chalets with the Matterhorn in view and an easy line to the lifts.
when: Zermatt's season runs from late November to early May, and its exceptional altitude gives it some of the most reliable snow in the Alps, with glacier skiing available year-round. It is a dependable choice at both ends of the season, while the depth of winter brings the fullest town. The Matterhorn is a constant, whatever the month. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: Because Zermatt is car-free, the arrival has a particular rhythm: guests drive or transfer to Tasch, at the foot of the valley, and take the short, scenic mountain train up into the resort. Geneva and Zurich are both around three and a half hours away by road to Tasch, and Zermatt's own rail links make a car-free trip genuinely easy. We arrange every leg, including a helicopter transfer up from the valley when you would rather skip the train.
beyond: Zermatt gives non-skiers and rest days a great deal. Simply strolling the car-free lanes, with electric taxis and horse-drawn sleighs and the Matterhorn ahead, is a pleasure, and there are glacier excursions, the Gornergrat railway and superb hotel spas for quieter days. The town's shopping and its grand-hotel life carry the evenings, and the scenery is never far from view. Non-skiers are thoroughly well served.
eating: Zermatt is famous for its on-mountain dining, a scattering of characterful huts serving everything from rosti to remarkable wine, and the town matches them with polished Swiss and international tables. The mountain restaurants are a destination in their own right, worth planning the day around, and the evening choice is broad and high in quality. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the ones worth planning your day around.
pick: Zermatt rewards those who take it slowly. Ski high and long beneath the Matterhorn, cross into Italy for a sunny lunch, and enjoy the car-free calm of the town in the evening. The train up, the tables and the timings, we will handle.

name: St. Moritz
intro: St. Moritz invented the winter holiday and has never stopped celebrating it. Set on a lake in the sun-drenched Upper Engadin, it pairs reliable high-altitude snow with a season of extravagant events on the frozen lake, from polo to horse racing, and a level of glamour that is entirely its own. It is a resort where the scene is as much the point as the skiing, and where the light, the altitude and the sense of occasion combine into something no other resort quite matches.
skiing: The skiing spreads across Corviglia, Corvatsch and Diavolezza, high, sunny and snow-sure, with the celebrated Corvatsch run and real glacier terrain for those who want it. The Upper Engadin's dry, bright climate keeps the pistes in excellent order, and the terrain suits confident intermediates and above especially well, with long, scenic descents and some serious off-piste. It is not the largest area in the Alps, but its quality, its altitude and its light set it apart. The skiing is high, sunny and rewarding, and it comes with a view over the lakes.
town: St. Moritz is really two parts: glamorous Dorf on the hillside, home to the grand hotels and the designer boutiques, and Bad by the lake, quieter and more spa-focused. Where you stay shapes the week, between the buzz of Dorf and the calm of Bad or the chalets above the town. The standard of property is exceptional, and the best of it comes with lake views and a sense of real occasion. Our own collection reflects that rarefied setting, from lake-view apartments to chalets above the town.
when: St. Moritz's season runs from late November to mid April, and its altitude and famously dry, sunny climate give it dependable snow and an unusual number of bright, blue days. The depth of winter brings the fullest social calendar, with the celebrated events out on the frozen lake, while the spring light on the Engadin is a particular pleasure. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: St. Moritz is reached by one of the most scenic rail journeys in the world, or a short flight to the local airfield. Zurich is around three hours away by road, and the Glacier and Bernina Express routes make the train a genuine part of the experience, climbing through the Engadin. The Samedan airfield, a few minutes from town, takes private aircraft directly. We arrange every transfer, whether by road, rail or air.
beyond: Few resorts offer as much off the snow as St. Moritz. The frozen lake hosts polo, cricket and horse racing through the winter, the historic Cresta Run offers a genuine thrill, and the grand-hotel spas provide the antidote, while the designer boutiques and the sense of occasion carry the evenings. It is a place where the social calendar and the setting are the point as much as the skiing. Non-skiers are gloriously well served.
eating: St. Moritz dines in style, from grand hotel restaurants to mountain huts with astonishing wine cellars, and the scene is as much about being seen as about the food. The mountain terraces reward a long, sunny lunch, and the evening choice is broad, glamorous and high in quality. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and secure the tables that matter.
pick: St. Moritz rewards those who embrace the occasion. Ski the high, sunny slopes above the lakes, take in one of the events out on the frozen lake, and give the evenings to the town's glamour. The rail journey, the tables and the timings, we will handle.

name: Gstaad
intro: Gstaad wears its wealth quietly. A strictly chalet-built village in the Bernese Oberland, it has drawn a discreet international set for generations with its promise of privacy, its horse-drawn sleighs and its gentle, unshowy pace. The skiing is spread across several friendly areas rather than one big domain, which suits the mood exactly; this is a resort for those who want understated luxury, a beautiful village and calm as much as vertical.
skiing: Gstaad's skiing is split across several sectors, mostly gentle and tree-lined and ideal for cruisers and families, with the Glacier 3000 area adding height and a little more challenge and snow-security. It is not a resort for those chasing steeps or big off-piste, and it does not pretend to be; what it offers is pleasant, scenic, low-pressure skiing to be enjoyed rather than conquered, spread over a wide and varied region. For a mixed group that values the setting and the ease over the challenge, it is close to ideal. The gentle terrain is very much part of the appeal.
town: Gstaad's strictly enforced chalet architecture gives the village a coherence and charm that few resorts match, and its car-limited promenade of boutiques and patisseries is a pleasure to wander. You can base yourself in or beside the village for the shops and the atmosphere, or in a secluded farmhouse in the surrounding valleys for real privacy. The chalet standard is exceptional and the emphasis is on discretion. Our own collection leans into that, from village-edge chalets to secluded farmhouses in the surrounding valleys.
when: Gstaad's season runs from mid December to mid April. Its altitude is modest across much of the area, so it relies on good snowmaking and the higher Glacier 3000 for security, but the tree-lined runs ski well in poor weather and the village's charm carries the quieter conditions. The holidays bring the fullest, most social weeks, while the longer spring days suit the resort's gentle pace. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Gstaad is a scenic and straightforward transfer from the main gateways. Geneva is around two hours away by road, with Zurich a little more, and the GoldenPass railway makes a lovely, unhurried alternative that suits the resort's mood. The village itself is easy to move around, much of it car-limited. We arrange every transfer, by road or rail.
beyond: Gstaad is made for the gentle day and the non-skier. The car-limited promenade, its boutiques and patisseries, is a pleasure to browse, and there are horse-drawn sleigh rides, winter walks and superb hotel spas for quiet afternoons. The emphasis throughout is on understated quality and calm rather than spectacle. Non-skiers are thoroughly at home.
eating: Gstaad keeps its dining understated but very good, from cosy chalet restaurants to the polished tables of its grand hotels. The mountain terraces reward a long, sunny lunch, and the village's discretion extends to its restaurants, which are excellent without shouting about it. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the ones worth knowing about.
pick: Gstaad rewards those who want calm and quality. Cruise the gentle, scenic slopes, give the afternoons to the village and its spas, and treat the discretion as the luxury it is. The sleighs, the spas and the tables, we will handle.

name: Davos
intro: Davos is less a resort than a mountain town, the highest in Europe, spread along a broad valley with five separate ski areas rising around it. Its scale and its sporting heritage give it real substance, and the legendary Parsenn, with its long descents towards Klosters, remains one of the great ski experiences in the Alps. It is a place for skiers who want a big, serious mountain and the amenities of a proper town rather than the charm of a small village.
skiing: Davos's five areas, Parsenn, Jakobshorn, Pischa, Rinerhorn and Madrisa, give more than 300km of varied terrain, from the long, classic Parsenn runs down towards Klosters to the freeride-friendly slopes of the Jakobshorn. Shared with Klosters, it is one of the largest areas in the eastern Alps, and its spread means you can ski a different mountain each day. There is plenty for every level, though the sheer scale rewards those who like to explore. The Parsenn descents alone justify the trip.
town: Davos is a substantial town rather than a village, which is its character: it offers the shopping, restaurants and life of a real place, spread along the valley, with the ski areas rising on either side. Where you base yourself matters, given the size, with the best positions close to the Parsenn or Jakobshorn lifts. It is more urban than pretty, but the chalet standard is high and the convenience considerable. Our own collection favours well-placed properties with quick access to the lifts and the quieter Klosters link.
when: Davos's season runs from late November to mid April, and its high position gives it a dependable snow record across the five areas. The depth of winter brings the fullest town, with its sporting events and its famous natural ice rink, while the longer spring days suit the sunny, open upper slopes. The scale means there is always a sheltered mountain to ski in poor weather. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: Davos is well connected by rail and road from Zurich, which is around two and a half hours away by car, and the Swiss rail network reaches the town comfortably, making a car-free trip straightforward. As a proper town, it is easy to move around once you arrive. We arrange every transfer, by road or rail.
beyond: As a real town, Davos has more to offer off the snow than most resorts. It has Europe's largest natural ice rink, a big sports centre, cross-country trails and museums, plus the shopping and life of a genuine town. It is substantial rather than quaint, and there is plenty to fill a non-skiing day at any pace. Non-skiers who like a town are especially well served.
eating: Being a proper town, Davos offers genuine range at the table, from traditional mountain huts to modern restaurants and a lively scene that owes as much to the town as to the slopes. The mountain terraces reward a long lunch with a view, and the evening choice is broad and varied. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and steer you to the tables worth booking.
pick: Davos rewards the explorer. Ski a different one of its five mountains each day, save the Parsenn's long descents to Klosters for a clear morning, and enjoy the amenities of a real town in the evening. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Grindelwald
intro: Grindelwald sits in a green bowl beneath the Eiger, Monch and Jungfrau, one of the most dramatic settings in the Alps. Part of the Jungfrau ski region, it combines gentle, scenic pistes with the extraordinary backdrop of the Eiger's north face and the mountain railways that made the area famous. It is a resort where the scenery does much of the work, and where families and cruisers can enjoy easy skiing in surroundings that few places on earth can match.
skiing: The Jungfrau region links Grindelwald with Wengen and Murren across some 200km of mostly gentle, panoramic pistes that suit families and cruisers especially well, with a few steeper pitches for variety. The First area offers sunny, open skiing above the village, while the runs beneath the Eiger and towards Wengen are long and scenic. New lifts have made the whole region quicker to get around. It is not a resort for those chasing steeps, but for scenery and for a mixed group it is hard to beat.
town: Grindelwald is a real village strung along its green valley, with the Eiger rising straight above it, and much of its appeal is simply the setting. The centre puts the shops, restaurants and railway within easy reach, while quieter positions up the hillsides make the most of the view. The chalet standard is high and the best properties frame the north face from the window. Our own collection favours chalets that make the most of that outlook, with easy access to the new lifts.
when: Grindelwald's season runs from mid December to mid April. Its altitude is modest, so it relies on good snowmaking lower down, but the higher Jungfrau slopes hold their snow and the scenery carries the quieter conditions. The holidays bring the busiest weeks, while the longer spring days suit the panoramic, sunny terrain. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Grindelwald is rail-connected through Interlaken, and the train journey in is a scenic pleasure. Zurich and Geneva are both around two and a half hours away by road, and the mountain railways make a car-free trip genuinely easy and part of the experience. The village is compact and simple to move around. We arrange every transfer, by road or rail.
beyond: Grindelwald's greatest asset off the snow is the scenery, and the train to the Jungfraujoch, the highest railway station in Europe, is a once-in-a-lifetime excursion. Beyond that there are long toboggan runs, winter walks and cable-car rides for spectacular non-skiing days, all beneath the Eiger. It is a resort where doing very little, but doing it in front of that view, is a pleasure in itself. Non-skiers are richly served.
eating: Grindelwald's mountain restaurants trade as much on their terraces and their outlook as on their cooking, which is warm and traditional, and a long lunch beneath the Eiger is a highlight in its own right. The village keeps its dining welcoming and unfussy, with the odd more ambitious table. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and recommend the best of them.
pick: Grindelwald rewards those who come for the scenery. Cruise the panoramic Jungfrau slopes, give a day to the train up to the Jungfraujoch, and take a long lunch on a terrace beneath the Eiger. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: St. Anton
intro: St. Anton is the spiritual home of alpine skiing, and it has never softened its edge. The largest resort in the Arlberg, it offers steep, demanding pistes, vast off-piste and a snow record that makes it a magnet for serious skiers, all wrapped up in an apres-ski scene of near-mythical intensity. It is a resort for those who want to ski hard and celebrate harder, and it does both with a commitment that few places can match.
skiing: St. Anton anchors the Arlberg's 305km of linked pistes, shared with Lech, Zurs and Warth, and it is best known for challenging terrain and enormous off-piste potential off the Valluga and the Rendl. It is not the gentlest resort, and it is honest about that; the pistes are demanding, the off-piste is world-class and the snow record is exceptional, which is exactly why the committed keep coming back. There is gentler terrain for mixed groups, but the mountain's heart belongs to the strong and adventurous skier. For those skiers, it is close to unbeatable.
town: St. Anton is a lively, characterful village strung along its valley, and it manages to be both a serious ski base and a place with real Tyrolean atmosphere. The centre puts you close to the lifts and to the famous apres, while quieter positions on the outskirts trade a little convenience for calm. The chalet standard is high and the best properties give quick access to the mountain and the village's energy. Our own collection favours chalets with quick access to the lifts and the village on tap.
when: St. Anton's season runs from early December to late April, and its position on the Arlberg gives it one of the best snow records in the Alps, with heavy, reliable falls a hallmark of the region. The depth of winter brings the fullest, most spirited village and the deepest snow, while the longer spring days suit the high, open terrain. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group, and their appetite for the apres in particular, and match the timing to it.
getting: St. Anton is rail-connected and close to two airports, which makes it one of the easier serious resorts to reach. Innsbruck is around an hour away by road and Zurich about two, and the village sits directly on the mainline railway, so a car-free trip is genuinely easy. That rail link, right into the heart of the village, is a real point of difference. We arrange every transfer, by road or rail.
beyond: Beyond the pistes, St. Anton has a large sports and wellness centre, tobogganing and winter walks, though it is, at heart, a place people come to ski hard and celebrate. The famous slope-side apres bars are an experience in themselves, and the village's Tyrolean character carries the evenings. It is more about energy than quiet charm, but non-skiers who like a lively resort will find plenty to enjoy. The apres is the headline off-slope draw.
eating: The mountain huts of St. Anton serve generous Austrian classics, and the descent home passes the notorious slope-side bars where the day's skiing tends to give way to the evening's dancing. The village has quieter, more refined tables too, from traditional Tyrolean rooms to the odd ambitious kitchen. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the calmer tables as well as pointing you to the legendary ones.
pick: St. Anton rewards the strong skier who likes to celebrate. Ski the Valluga and the off-piste hard while the snow is best, brace yourself for the slope-side apres on the way home, and let the village's energy carry the evening. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Lech
intro: Lech is the Arlberg at its most refined, a snowy, upmarket village that has long drawn royalty and a discreet international set. It shares the vast Arlberg ski area but keeps its own polished character, with famously reliable snow and the celebrated Weisse Ring circuit looping around the mountains above the village. It is a resort for those who want serious Arlberg skiing with a calmer, more exclusive village at the end of the day.
skiing: Lech's slopes are beautifully maintained and, thanks to its position, exceptionally snowy, with the Weisse Ring offering a satisfying full-day circuit around the mountains and links across the Arlberg's 305km. There is off-piste for the adventurous and gentle, well-groomed cruising for everyone else, so it suits a broad range while keeping the quality high. The snow record is among the best in the Alps. It is Arlberg skiing with a more refined edge than neighbouring St. Anton.
town: Lech is a snowy, upmarket village of real charm, quieter and more exclusive than St. Anton, and it wears its status discreetly. The centre puts the best of the hotels, shops and restaurants within reach, while quieter positions and neighbouring Zurs offer even greater calm. The chalet standard is exceptional and the emphasis is on quiet quality. Our own collection favours the finest chalets close to the village and the lifts.
when: Lech's season runs from early December to late April, and its position makes it one of the snowiest resorts in the Alps, with heavy, reliable falls throughout. The depth of winter brings the deepest snow and the fullest village, while the longer spring days suit the high, open circuit skiing. The reliable cover is a hallmark of the resort. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: Lech is a short transfer from Innsbruck or Zurich. Zurich is around two hours away by road and Innsbruck a little over an hour, with St. Anton the nearest railway station, a short drive down the pass. The village itself is calm and easy to move around. We arrange every transfer, by road or rail.
beyond: Lech offers superb hotel spas, sleigh rides and winter walks in a genuinely upmarket setting, with the emphasis on quiet quality rather than spectacle. It is a place to ski well and rest well, and the village's discretion extends to its diversions. Non-skiers are thoroughly indulged. The refined calm is the point.
eating: Lech dines exceptionally well, with a notable concentration of fine restaurants and elegant mountain huts serving refined Austrian cooking. The mountain terraces reward a long, sunny lunch, and the village's evening choice is polished and high in quality. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and secure the tables that fill fastest.
pick: Lech rewards those who want serious skiing and a refined village. Ski the Weisse Ring circuit and the deep Arlberg snow, then return to a village that does quiet quality better than almost anywhere. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Kitzbuhel
intro: Kitzbuhel is one of the loveliest towns in the Alps, a walled medieval centre of frescoed houses, smart boutiques and genuine buzz. Each January it hosts the Hahnenkamm, whose fearsome Streif is the most celebrated and feared downhill on the World Cup calendar, and the town's glamour and history keep it busy long after the racers leave. It is a resort where a beautiful, lively town is as much the draw as the skiing itself.
skiing: KitzSki offers around 230km of well-connected, mostly intermediate pistes across a beautiful landscape, from the racing legend of the Hahnenkamm to gentle cruising elsewhere. Its relatively low altitude is offset by some of the best snowmaking in the Alps, which keeps the pistes in good order, though the season can be more weather-dependent than higher resorts. There is enough variety for a mixed group, and the setting is glorious. It is skiing to be enjoyed at a civilised pace, with a beautiful town at the bottom.
town: Kitzbuhel's medieval town is the real attraction, a genuinely beautiful walled centre of cobbled streets, frescoed houses and smart shops, alive with a cosmopolitan crowd. You can base yourself in or beside the old town for the atmosphere and the shopping, or in a quieter position on the outskirts. The chalet standard is high and the town's glamour is a large part of the appeal. Our own collection favours chalets and residences with easy reach of the town and the lifts.
when: Kitzbuhel's season runs from mid December to early April, and its lower altitude means it leans on excellent snowmaking, so the timing matters a little more than at higher resorts. The depth of winter, and the Hahnenkamm weekend in January, bring the busiest and most glamorous weeks, while the town's beauty carries the quieter conditions. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Kitzbuhel is close to three airports, which makes it easy to reach. Innsbruck and Salzburg are both around an hour to ninety minutes away by road, with Munich a little further, and the town has its own railway station, so a car-free trip is straightforward. That accessibility suits a short glamorous break as well as a full week. We arrange every transfer, by road or rail.
beyond: The town itself is Kitzbuhel's great asset off the snow, with its boutiques, its casino, its galleries and its grand-hotel spas, plus winter walks and tobogganing nearby. It is a resort where the shopping, the eating and the sheer beauty of the medieval centre are the point as much as the skiing. Non-skiers are exceptionally well served. The town carries the quieter days effortlessly.
eating: For a small town, Kitzbuhel dines with real range and style, from traditional Tyrolean rooms to smart modern restaurants and a lively bar scene. The mountain huts reward a long lunch, and the town's evening choice is broad and glamorous. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the tables worth planning around.
pick: Kitzbuhel rewards those who love a beautiful town. Cruise the scenic pistes, give the afternoons to the medieval centre and its shops, and treat the town's glamour as part of the holiday. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Ischgl
intro: Ischgl combines a genuinely excellent, high-altitude ski area with a reputation for the most exuberant nightlife in the Alps. Linked across the border to duty-free Samnaun in Switzerland, its snow-sure pistes draw serious skiers, while its season-opening and closing concerts draw some of the biggest names in music. It is a resort for those who want reliable, high skiing by day and a proper party by night, and it delivers both with real conviction.
skiing: The Silvretta Arena's 239km sit high and hold their snow beautifully, a well-linked spread of mostly intermediate and advanced pistes with excellent lift infrastructure. The cross-border trip to Samnaun, and its duty-free lunch, is an Ischgl tradition, and the sheer quality of the grooming and the snow record set the resort apart. There is enough here for a strong, mixed group to cover a lot of ground. The high, snow-reliable terrain is the substance beneath the party reputation.
town: Ischgl is a compact, lively village built around its gondolas, and it is designed for quick access to the skiing and to the famous nightlife. The centre puts you close to both, while quieter positions on the edge offer a little more calm. It is more functional than picture-postcard, but the best chalets and residences give easy access to the lifts. Our own collection favours comfortable properties close to the gondolas.
when: Ischgl's season runs from late November to early May, and its altitude gives it one of the most reliable snow records in Austria, dependable at both ends of the winter. The famous concerts bookend the season, and the depth of winter brings the fullest, liveliest village. The high terrain can be exposed in poor weather. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: Ischgl is a comfortable transfer from Innsbruck or Zurich. Innsbruck is around ninety minutes away by road and Zurich about two and a half hours, with the final approach up the Paznaun valley. The village is compact and easy to move around once you arrive. We arrange every transfer.
beyond: Ischgl's famous open-air concerts bookend the season, and there is a large spa complex and the novelty of a duty-free ski across the border into Samnaun. It is a lively base, more about energy and nightlife than quiet charm, though calmer corners and the spa exist for rest days. Non-skiers who like a lively resort will find plenty to enjoy. The concerts and the nightlife are the headline draw.
eating: The mountain huts of Ischgl serve hearty Austrian cooking, and the slope-side bars are the stuff of legend, though the village also has quieter, more refined tables. The cross-border lunch in duty-free Samnaun is a tradition worth keeping. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book whichever mood you are in.
pick: Ischgl rewards the group that wants big skiing and a big night. Ski the snow-sure Silvretta Arena hard, take the duty-free lunch over the border in Samnaun, and let the famous nightlife carry the evening. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Solden
intro: Solden is one of Austria's highest and most snow-sure resorts, a big, energetic destination in the Otztal with two glaciers and three lift-served peaks above 3,000m. Its dramatic high-mountain terrain has featured on the World Cup circuit and the cinema screen alike, giving it a bold, modern character. It is a resort for those who want guaranteed snow, high-alpine scale and a lively atmosphere, and it delivers all three with confidence.
skiing: Solden's 144km climb high onto the Rettenbach and Tiefenbach glaciers, giving snow-security at both ends of the season and long, satisfying descents from three peaks above 3,000m. The terrain suits confident intermediates and above particularly well, with plenty of length and vertical, and the glaciers guarantee good conditions when lower resorts struggle. It is a big, high mountain with a bold character. The altitude and the scale are the headline.
town: Solden is a substantial, lively resort strung along the Otztal valley, built more for energy and convenience than for traditional charm. The centre puts you close to the gondolas and the nightlife, while quieter positions offer a little more calm. The best chalets and residences give quick access to the lifts. Our own collection favours well-placed properties close to the gondolas.
when: Solden's season runs from November to early May on the glaciers, one of the longest and most reliable in Austria. It is a dependable choice for an early or late trip, while the depth of winter brings the fullest, liveliest resort. The high terrain can be exposed in poor weather. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: Solden is a straightforward run from Innsbruck, which is around an hour away by road, with Munich about two and a half hours. The approach climbs the Otztal valley to the resort. The village is easy to move around once you arrive. We arrange every transfer.
beyond: Solden's diversions suit its bold, high-altitude character: there is a striking mountain-top attraction devoted to a certain secret agent, plus spas and glacier excursions, and a lively nightlife scene. It is more about energy and spectacle than quiet charm, and the scenery does much of the work. Non-skiers who like an active, lively resort are well served.
eating: From glacier-top restaurants with remarkable views to hearty village tables and a spirited bar scene, Solden covers the range. The mountain terraces reward a long, high-altitude lunch, and the village has real energy in the evening. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and recommend the tables worth booking.
pick: Solden rewards the group that wants high, snow-sure skiing with energy. Ski the glaciers and the 3,000m peaks, take a long lunch with a view from the top, and enjoy the resort's lively evenings. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Saalbach-Hinterglemm
intro: Saalbach-Hinterglemm anchors the Skicircus, a huge, well-linked area that lets you ski a full circuit around the valley and back to your door. Sunny, welcoming and sociable, it pairs a great spread of mostly intermediate terrain with a deserved reputation for cheerful, slope-side apres. It is a resort for those who like to cover ground by day and enjoy a lively, good-natured evening, and it does that combination as well as anywhere in Austria.
skiing: The Skicircus links Saalbach and Hinterglemm with Leogang and Fieberbrunn across 270km, and its signature is the full-day circuit that returns you to the start without retracing your steps. The terrain is mostly intermediate, sunny and confidence-building, which makes it ideal for mixed groups who like to keep moving, with a few steeper pitches and some off-piste for variety. The sheer connectedness of the area is its great strength. It is skiing built for covering ground and keeping a varied party happy.
town: Saalbach and Hinterglemm are two linked villages along a sunny valley, both cheerful and welcoming, with a good-natured, sociable atmosphere. The centres put you close to the lifts and the apres, while quieter positions offer a little more calm. It is more lively than refined, but the best chalets give easy access to the slopes and the action. Our own collection favours comfortable chalets close to the lifts.
when: Saalbach's season runs from early December to mid April, and its sunny aspect and good snowmaking keep the circuit skiing in good order, though the timing matters a little more than at the highest resorts. The depth of winter brings the fullest, liveliest weeks, while the longer spring days suit the sunny terraces and the apres. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Saalbach is one of the closer resorts to Salzburg, which is around ninety minutes away by road, with Innsbruck a similar distance. The approach is straightforward and the villages easy to move around. We arrange every transfer.
beyond: Beyond the pistes, Saalbach offers long toboggan runs, a high-ropes course and plenty of sunny terraces, all in a relaxed, friendly setting. The mood throughout is cheerful and sociable rather than refined, and the slope-side apres is part of the experience. Non-skiers and rest-day skiers who like a lively resort are well served.
eating: The mountain huts and village restaurants of Saalbach serve generous Austrian cooking, and the slope-side bars are part of the experience, cheerful and sociable. The sunny terraces reward a long lunch, and the evening is lively and easy-going. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and point you to the tables worth booking.
pick: Saalbach rewards the group that likes to cover ground and enjoy the evening. Ski the full Skicircus circuit, take a long lunch on a sunny terrace, and let the good-natured apres carry the evening. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Cortina d'Ampezzo
intro: Cortina d'Ampezzo is the grande dame of the Italian Alps, a handsome town set in a wide, sunlit bowl and ringed by the sheer, rose-tinted towers of the Dolomites, some of the most extraordinary mountain scenery anywhere in the skiing world. It has been a byword for Italian style and glamour for the best part of a century, and it returns to the world stage as a host of the 2026 Winter Olympics. For all its social reputation, though, Cortina is at heart a relaxed and deeply scenic place, as much about the aperitivo and the evening passeggiata as about the vertical.
skiing: Cortina's own slopes are broad, sunny and mostly gentle to intermediate, and they ski as much for the setting as for the challenge; long, cruising pistes wind down beneath the Tofane, the Cristallo and the Faloria, with the scenery a constant distraction. The resort is famous for cinematic descents rather than for steeps, though there is enough off-piste and a handful of testing runs, the Olympic pistes among them, to keep strong skiers interested. Crucially, Cortina forms part of Dolomiti Superski, the largest linked lift network in the world, so a single pass opens an almost limitless spread of terrain, with the Sella Ronda circuit within reach for a full day's touring. It suits mixed groups and confident cruisers especially well.
town: Cortina is a real town rather than a resort built to a plan, and it wears its elegance lightly. The pedestrian Corso Italia, lined with boutiques, cafes and the daily ritual of the passeggiata, is the heart of it, while the town spreads out into quieter hamlets around the valley. That range lets you choose between the buzz of the centre and the seclusion of a chalet with the Dolomites filling the windows. The standard of property is high, and because the skiing is spread across several sectors, where you base yourself shapes the week. Our own collection is short and considered, and we match the chalet to the group.
when: Cortina's winter runs from early December through to April, and its character shifts across the season. The Dolomites enjoy a famously sunny, dry climate, so bright, blue days are common; the trade-off is that Cortina leans on excellent snowmaking to supplement the natural cover, which it does to a very high standard. The depth of winter brings the fullest social calendar, while the longer, warmer days towards the end of the season are made for long lunches on the mountain terraces. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Cortina is more remote than the big French and Swiss resorts, and that seclusion is part of its appeal, but it remains straightforward to reach. Venice is the usual gateway, around two hours away by road, with Treviso a similar distance and often the easier arrival; Innsbruck and Verona are the other sensible options, each a little further. There is no airport or railway station in Cortina itself, so the final approach is always by road, a scenic climb through the foothills.
