As part of any stay we arrange every transfer, and can add a helicopter leg from Venice when time is short, because the drive, while beautiful, is not a quick one.
beyond: Few resorts reward a non-skier, or a rest day, as richly as Cortina. The town is made for wandering, with its boutiques, galleries and cafes, and the evening passeggiata along the Corso is an event in its own right. Beyond the shopping there are excellent hotel spas, the drama of the Dolomite scenery from every cable car, and the bob and skeleton heritage of past and future Olympic Games. Winter walking trails, sleigh rides and long lunches on sun-drenched terraces fill the gentler days, and the mountains never quite become ordinary.
eating: Cortina takes its food and its long lunches every bit as seriously as its skiing. On the mountain, a scattering of characterful rifugi reward a slow, sunny lunch of proper Italian cooking with a Dolomite view; down in the town, the range runs from unfussy trattorie to genuinely ambitious dining rooms, and the evening tends to open with an aperitivo rather than end on the dance floor. The apres is more about style than spectacle. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the tables that are hardest to get.
pick: Cortina rewards those who slow down. Ski the sunny cruising beneath the Tofane in the morning, take a long lunch at a mountain rifugio while the light moves across the Dolomites, and give one evening to the Corso and a proper table in town. The transfer, the tables and the timings, we will handle.

name: Courmayeur
intro: Courmayeur sits on the southern, Italian side of Mont Blanc, a handsome medieval town of cobbled streets and mountaineering heritage. Its skiing is sunny and scenic, its off-piste serious, and its mountain lunches are among the best in the Alps, all just through the Mont Blanc tunnel from Chamonix. It is a resort that pairs genuine Italian style with big-mountain terrain, and it does the long, elegant lunch as well as anywhere.
skiing: Courmayeur's own pistes are sunny, scenic and mostly intermediate, but the real draw for strong skiers is the off-piste, especially the celebrated descents from the Italian side of the Mont Blanc massif, which are world-class and best taken with a guide. The Skyway cable car to Punta Helbronner is a spectacle in its own right, opening up serious high-alpine terrain. The pisted area is compact, but the character and the off-piste potential are considerable. It is a mountain that rewards the adventurous and flatters the cruiser in equal measure.
town: Courmayeur is a genuine medieval town rather than a purpose-built resort, with a handsome, cobbled centre and real Italian style. The town sits across the valley from the main lifts, reached by a short cable car or transfer, so where you base yourself shapes the rhythm of the week. The chalet standard is high and the town's charm is a large part of the appeal. Our own collection favours chalets with the massif in view and the old town within reach.
when: Courmayeur's season runs from early December to mid April, and its high, north-facing off-piste holds cold snow well, while the pisted slopes catch the southern sun. The depth of winter brings the coldest, most reliable conditions for the big descents, while the longer spring days suit the sunny terraces and the long lunch. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group, and their appetite for off-piste, and match the timing to it.
getting: Courmayeur is quick to reach from Geneva and Turin. Geneva is around ninety minutes away by road via the Mont Blanc tunnel, with Turin a similar distance, which makes it one of the easier Italian resorts to get to. The town sits at the foot of the massif, an easy transfer from the tunnel. We arrange every transfer.
beyond: Off the snow, Courmayeur offers the spectacular Skyway Monte Bianco cable car, the Pre-Saint-Didier thermal baths and a handsome old town that is a pleasure to explore. It is a resort where the scenery, the shopping and the eating are the point as much as the skiing. Non-skiers are well looked after, and the town's Italian style carries the evenings.
eating: The mountain rifugi of Courmayeur are legendary, serving proper Italian cooking with Mont Blanc filling the view, and a long lunch on the mountain is a highlight in its own right. The town's restaurants and wine bars are every bit as good, with real Italian style. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the tables that matter.
pick: Courmayeur rewards the adventurous who also love to eat. Take a guided off-piste day from the Skyway, keep a long lunch at a mountain rifugio with a Mont Blanc view, and give the evenings to the old town. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Alta Badia
intro: Alta Badia is a cluster of villages in a quiet Dolomite valley, the heartland of the Ladin culture and one of the great gastronomic corners of the Alps. Its gentle, immaculately groomed pistes form part of the Sella Ronda circuit and the vast Dolomiti Superski, making it a paradise for cruisers who like to eat well. It is a resort where the food, the scenery and the easy skiing combine into something genuinely distinctive.
skiing: Alta Badia's beautifully groomed, mostly gentle pistes are ideal for cruising, and they link directly into the Sella Ronda, the famous circuit around the Sella massif, and the wider 1,200km of Dolomiti Superski. It is not a resort for those chasing steeps, and it is honest about that; what it offers is immaculate, scenic, low-pressure skiing over an enormous linked area, with the Dolomite scenery a constant reward. It is skiing to be savoured rather than conquered. The sheer scale of the linked area, on a single pass, is remarkable.
town: Alta Badia is a scatter of quiet, characterful Ladin villages, Corvara, La Villa, San Cassiano and others, each with its own feel and all sharing the same beautiful, food-focused calm. Where you base yourself shapes the week a little, but all sit within easy reach of the Sella Ronda lifts. The chalet standard is high and the emphasis is on quiet quality. Our own collection favours characterful chalets in the prettiest of the villages.
when: Alta Badia's season runs from early December to mid April, and the Dolomites' sunny, dry climate gives it plenty of bright days, backed by excellent snowmaking on the mostly gentle terrain. The depth of winter brings the fullest villages, while the longer spring days suit the sunny cruising and the long lunch. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Alta Badia is reached via Venice, Verona or Innsbruck. Innsbruck and Venice are both around two to two and a half hours away by road, with Verona a similar distance, and the final approach climbs into the Dolomite valleys. The villages are quiet and easy to move around. We arrange every transfer.
beyond: Off the snow, Alta Badia is defined by its distinctive Ladin culture, its museums, its spas and its gentle winter walks, all in a quiet, food-focused setting. It is a restful, civilised base where doing very little, and eating very well, is a pleasure in itself. Non-skiers who value calm and cuisine are especially well served.
eating: Alta Badia is famous for its mountain dining, with several Michelin-recognised kitchens and a run of rifugi serving refined Ladin and Italian cooking against a Dolomite backdrop. A long lunch on the mountain here is a genuine highlight, and the villages match it in the evening. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and secure the tables worth the trip.
pick: Alta Badia rewards those who ski to eat. Cruise the immaculate Sella Ronda pistes, plan the day around a long lunch at a celebrated mountain rifugio, and settle into the quiet Ladin calm in the evening. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Madonna di Campiglio
intro: Madonna di Campiglio is one of Italy's smartest resorts, set on a high plateau between the Brenta Dolomites and the Adamello glaciers. Its beautifully groomed pistes, elegant centre and fashionable clientele give it a distinctly Italian glamour, with skiing to match for cruisers and confident intermediates. It is a resort where style and immaculate skiing combine in an unforgettable Dolomite setting.
skiing: Around 150km of superbly maintained pistes link Campiglio with Pinzolo and Folgarida-Marilleva, mostly gentle to intermediate with a few testing runs, including a former World Cup night slalom. The grooming is immaculate and the setting, beneath the Brenta towers, is spectacular. It is skiing built for cruising and for enjoying the scenery, with enough variety to keep a mixed group happy. The elegance of the pistes matches the elegance of the resort.
town: Madonna di Campiglio has an elegant, pedestrian centre that is a pleasure to wander, lined with smart shops and cafes, and the evening passeggiata is part of the ritual. You can base yourself in or beside the centre for the atmosphere, or in a quieter position on the plateau. The chalet standard is high and the resort's polish is a large part of the appeal. Our own collection favours stylish chalets and residences close to the lifts.
when: Madonna's season runs from early December to mid April, with the high plateau and good snowmaking keeping the immaculate pistes in order, backed by the Adamello glaciers for security. The depth of winter brings the fullest, most fashionable weeks, while the longer spring days suit the sunny terraces. Rather than single out a month, we would rather understand your group and match the week to it.
getting: Madonna di Campiglio is reached via Verona or Milan. Verona is around two hours away by road, with Milan and Innsbruck a little further, and the final approach climbs onto the high plateau. The centre is pedestrian and easy to move around. We arrange every transfer.
beyond: Off the snow, Madonna's pedestrian centre, its boutiques and cafes, plus hotel spas and gentle winter walks, give non-skiers a stylish, easy time. The scenery beneath the Brenta is a constant pleasure, and the resort's elegance carries the evenings. Non-skiers are well and stylishly served.
eating: Madonna dines with real elegance, from mountain rifugi to smart restaurants in the centre, and the evening passeggiata often ends with an aperitivo before dinner. The mountain terraces reward a long, sunny lunch, and the town's evening choice is polished and Italian. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and book the tables worth knowing.
pick: Madonna di Campiglio rewards those who like style with their skiing. Cruise the immaculate pistes beneath the Brenta, give the afternoons to the elegant centre, and enjoy the resort's Italian polish in the evening. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.

name: Cervinia
intro: Breuil-Cervinia lies at the foot of the Italian side of the Matterhorn, one of the highest and most snow-sure resorts in the country. Its long, sunny, gentle pistes make it a cruiser's delight, and a lift link over the ridge to Zermatt lets you ski between Italy and Switzerland in a single, memorable day. It is a resort for those who want guaranteed snow, easy, scenic skiing and Italian value beneath one of the great mountains.
skiing: Cervinia's terrain is high, sunny and mostly gentle, with some of the longest cruising runs in the Alps and a dependable snow record thanks to the altitude, which makes it a strong choice for improvers and for early or late-season trips. The connection over the ridge to Zermatt adds steeper Swiss terrain and a cross-border lunch with a view of the Matterhorn from both sides. It is not a resort for those chasing steeps on the Italian side, but the length, the sunshine and the snow-security are the draw. The combined area with Zermatt is enormous.
town: Cervinia is a high, purpose-built resort rather than a traditional village, functional and geared to ski-in convenience and the snow. The centre puts you close to the lifts, and the best chalets and residences make the most of the position and the Matterhorn views. It trades charm for altitude and access, which for a snow-focused group is a fair exchange. Our own collection favours comfortable properties with quick access to the lifts.
when: Cervinia's season runs from late November to early May, and its exceptional altitude gives it one of the most reliable snow records in Italy, dependable at both ends of the winter. It is a natural choice for an early or late trip, while the depth of winter brings the fullest resort. The high, open terrain can be exposed in poor weather, when crossing to Zermatt's sheltered runs is the answer. Rather than single out a week, we would rather understand your group and match the timing to it.
getting: Cervinia is reached via Turin or Geneva. Turin is around two hours away by road, with Geneva and Milan a little further, and the final approach climbs the valley to the resort at the foot of the Matterhorn. The village is compact and easy to move around. We arrange every transfer.
beyond: Off the snow, Cervinia's reliable sunshine, its hotel spas and the ever-present Matterhorn make for easy rest days, with the option of a scenic trip over to Zermatt for a change of scene. It is more functional than charming, but the setting and the snow-security are the point. Non-skiers will find enough to enjoy, especially with Zermatt a lift ride away.
eating: The mountain rifugi of Cervinia serve proper Italian cooking on sun-drenched terraces, and the resort's restaurants are warm, welcoming and good value. A long, sunny lunch on the mountain is a highlight, and the cross-border option in Zermatt adds a grander alternative. We keep our specific recommendations current through the concierge team rather than in print, and recommend the tables worth booking.
pick: Cervinia rewards those who want sun, snow and long, easy runs. Cruise the high pistes beneath the Matterhorn, cross into Zermatt for a memorable lunch with a view, and enjoy the Italian value in the evening. The transfers and the tables, we will handle.
